---
title: "3.2.3 Sensors"
subtitle: "Control systems · Hardware P114-P118"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <style>
        .reveal .term { color: #cf1f2e; font-weight: 700; }
        .reveal .mark { background: #fff36d; padding: 0 0.08em; }
        .reveal .tight-list li { margin-bottom: 0.24em; }
        .reveal .figure-note { font-size: 0.82em; line-height: 1.28; }
        </style>
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 3 Directory / 返回 Unit 3 目录](../../index.html)

## Control Systems

### Automatic action

Sensors are used in both monitoring and control applications.

In a <span class="term">control system</span>, if the <span class="mark">new data is outside the acceptable range</span>, the computer/microprocessor sends signals to <span class="term">control devices</span>, such as valves, motors, heaters or pumps.

## Figure 3.50

### Control path

![Figure 3.50 Monitoring and control systems using sensors](../../assets/3-hardware/3-2-3-sensors-control-systems-p114-p118/Figure 3.50 Monitoring and control systems using sensors.png){fig-align="center" width="94%"}

::: {.figure-note}
Figure 3.50 is a simplification of the process. Use the right-hand side for control: sensor input, ADC if necessary, comparison with stored values, output to devices, and feedback from the changed process.
:::

## Control Sequence 1/2

### Input and analysis

::: {.tight-list}
- <span class="term">sensors</span> send signals to the microprocessor/computer
- the signals are converted to digital, if necessary, using an <span class="term">analogue to digital converter (ADC)</span>
- the computer/microprocessor analyses the data received
- the data is checked against <span class="term">stored values</span>
:::

## Control Sequence 2/2

### Output and feedback

::: {.tight-list}
- if the new data is <span class="mark">outside the acceptable range</span>, the microprocessor/computer sends signals to control <span class="term">valves</span>, <span class="term">motors</span> and other devices
- a <span class="term">DAC</span> may be needed later in the output route if the controlled device needs an analogue signal
- an <span class="term">actuator</span> may be used to operate a physical device
- the output from the system affects the next set of inputs from the sensors
:::

## Feedback Loop

### The changed process becomes the next input

The output from the control system changes the process being controlled.

That changed process then affects the next set of sensor readings.

This repeated route is the <span class="term">feedback loop</span>.

## Examples of Control Path

### Figure 3.50 right-hand route

The control path follows the right-hand side of Figure 3.50:

::: {.tight-list}
- sensors send signals to the microprocessor/computer
- signals are converted to digital if necessary using an <span class="term">ADC</span>
- the microprocessor/computer analyses the data by checking it against stored values
- if the new data is outside the acceptable range, the microprocessor/computer sends signals to control <span class="term">valves</span>, <span class="term">motors</span> or other devices
- the output from the system affects the next set of inputs from the sensors
:::

This last step is the <span class="term">feedback loop</span>.

## Monitoring and Control

### The difference

Monitoring sends a warning message or activates an alarm; the microprocessor/computer has <span class="mark">no effect on what is being monitored</span>.

Control sends signals to devices such as valves, motors, heaters or pumps, so the process is changed automatically.

## Examples of Control

### Textbook examples

::: {.tight-list}
- turning <span class="term">street lights</span> on at night and off during daylight
- controlling the temperature in a <span class="term">central heating</span> or air conditioning system
- <span class="term">chemical process</span> control, for example maintaining temperature and pH
- operating <span class="term">anti-lock brakes</span> on a car when necessary
- controlling the environment in a <span class="term">green house</span> / <span class="term">greenhouse</span>
:::

## Application Pattern

### Same structure in each context

Every control application in the next deck follows the same answer pattern:

<span class="mark">sensor</span> → <span class="mark">ADC</span> → <span class="mark">computer comparison</span> → <span class="mark">DAC if needed</span> → <span class="mark">actuator/output device</span> → <span class="mark">feedback loop</span>.

The details change, but the control logic stays the same: name the sensor, the stored value or pre-set value, the comparison, the device controlled, and how the changed output affects later readings.

## Classroom Check

### Do not stop at the output

A strong answer should include:

::: {.tight-list}
- the sensor and measured property
- <span class="term">ADC</span> before the computer if the sensor data is analogue
- comparison with <span class="term">stored values</span> or pre-set values
- <span class="term">DAC</span> and <span class="term">actuator</span> where the output device needs them
- the <span class="term">feedback loop</span>, where the output affects the next sensor input
:::

## End

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 3 Directory / 返回 Unit 3 目录](../../index.html)
